content:
# Genesis Chapter 46 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27r–27v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_046_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_046_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 46–47 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050

---

## The Journey to Beersheba: The Last Patriarchal Vision

**1** So Israel set out with all that he had, and he came to Beersheba, and he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.

**2** And God spoke to Israel in visions of the night and said, "Jacob, Jacob!" And he said, "Here I am."

**3** And he said, "I am God, the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for I will make you into a great nation there.

**4** I myself will go down with you to Egypt, and I myself will also bring you up again; and Joseph's hand shall close your eyes."

---

## The Departure from Canaan

**5** Then Jacob set out from Beersheba, and the sons of Israel carried Jacob their father, and their little ones, and their wives, in the wagons that Pharaoh had sent to carry him.

**6** And they took their livestock and their possessions, which they had gained in the land of Canaan, and they came to Egypt — Jacob and all his offspring with him:

**7** his sons, and his sons' sons with him, his daughters, and his sons' daughters — all his offspring he brought with him to Egypt.

---

## The Register of Those Who Went to Egypt

**8** Now these are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt — Jacob and his sons:

Reuben, Jacob's firstborn.

**9** And the sons of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.

**10** And the sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman.

**11** And the sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.

**12** And the sons of Judah: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah — but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan. And the sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul.

**13** And the sons of Issachar: Tola, Puvah, Jashub, and Shimron.

**14** And the sons of Zebulun: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.

**15** These are the sons of Leah, whom she bore to Jacob in Paddan-aram, together with his daughter Dinah; all the persons of his sons and his daughters: thirty-three.

**16** And the sons of Gad: Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.

**17** And the sons of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, and Beriah, and Serah their sister. And the sons of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.

**18** These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah his daughter; and she bore these to Jacob: sixteen persons.

**19** The sons of Rachel, Jacob's wife: Joseph and Benjamin.

**20** And to Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath the daughter of Potiphera the priest of On bore to him.

**21** And the sons of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.

**22** These are the sons of Rachel who were born to Jacob: fourteen persons in all.

**23** And the sons of Dan: Hushim.

**24** And the sons of Naphtali: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.

**25** These are the sons of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to Rachel his daughter, and she bore these to Jacob: seven persons in all.

**26** All the persons belonging to Jacob who came to Egypt, those who came from his own body, besides the wives of Jacob's sons, were sixty-six persons in all.

**27** And the sons of Joseph who were born to him in Egypt were two persons. All the persons of the house of Jacob who came to Egypt: seventy.

---

## Arrival in Goshen

**28** And he sent Judah ahead of him to Joseph, to show the way before him to Goshen; and they came to the land of Goshen.

**29** Then Joseph harnessed his chariot and went up to meet Israel his father in Goshen. And he appeared to him and fell on his neck and wept on his neck a good while.

**30** And Israel said to Joseph, "Now let me die, since I have seen your face — that you are still alive."

---

## Joseph's Instructions about Goshen

**31** Then Joseph said to his brothers and to his father's household, "I will go up and tell Pharaoh, and I will say to him, 'My brothers and my father's household, who were in the land of Canaan, have come to me.

**32** And the men are shepherds, for they have been keepers of livestock, and they have brought their flocks and their herds and all that they have.'

**33** And when Pharaoh calls you and says, 'What is your occupation?'

**34** you shall say, 'Your servants have been keepers of livestock from our youth until now, both we and our fathers' — so that you may dwell in the land of Goshen, for every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt."

---

## Translation Notes

**Note 1 — v. 1: "Israel" and "Jacob" — the name alternation**
The chapter opens with "Israel" (wayyissa' Yisra'el — "so Israel set out"), a name that signals the patriarch acting as the covenant people collectively. But in v. 2, God addresses him as "Jacob, Jacob" (Ya'akov Ya'akov) — the doubled name of urgent personal summons, as with Abraham (Gen 22:11) and Moses (Exod 3:4). The response "Here I am" (hinneni) belongs to Jacob the individual man, not to Israel the nation. The alternation across vv. 5–8 (Jacob, Israel, Jacob, Israel) mirrors this theological duality: Jacob is the man making the journey; Israel is the nation being constituted. The translation preserves both names without harmonizing them.

**Note 2 — v. 2: "visions of the night" (mar'ot ha-laylah)**
Not a dream (halom) but "visions" (mar'ot, plural of mar'eh). This is the same visionary mode used with Abraham (Gen 15:1) and Balaam (Num 24:4, 16). The plural "visions" is maintained over the singular to preserve this distinction. This is the final theophanic address to a patriarch in Genesis — the book closes with divine speech.

**Note 3 — v. 3: "I am God, the God of your father" (El ha-Elohei avikha)**
The title is El — not El Shaddai as in 17:1 and 35:11, not YHWH, but the simple and ancient divine name El paired with "the God of your father." This ties back to the Beersheba altar of Isaac (26:24-25) and to the Elohei avikha formula common to patriarchal promise speech. The phrasing "the God of your father" is singular ("father," not "fathers"), pointing to Isaac specifically — Jacob has just come from the territory of his father's altar.

**Note 4 — v. 4: "I myself will go down... I myself will bring you up" (anokhi ered... anokhi a'alekha)**
The emphatic pronoun anokhi — "I myself" — appears twice, framing the divine promise. Both are rendered with "I myself" to preserve the weight of personal divine accompaniment. The verb "bring you up" (a'alekha) is the same root used for the Exodus (Exod 3:8, 17): the promise to Jacob here is proleptically the promise of the Exodus itself.

**Note 5 — v. 4: "Joseph's hand shall close your eyes" (yashit yado al-einekha)**
Literally "Joseph will put his hand upon your eyes." This is an ancient gesture of closing the eyes of the dying — to be present at the moment of death, to perform the last act of care. "Close your eyes" renders this idiom in natural English while preserving the intimacy of the image. The promise is fulfilled in 50:1.

**Note 6 — v. 15: "thirty-three" — Leah's count**
The MT arrives at 33 persons for Leah's line (vv. 8–15), but the arithmetic requires counting Jacob himself along with the listed 32 descendants. Some scholars count only 32 direct descendants. The MT figure of 33 is retained. The LXX counts 33 here as well but adds five grandsons of Joseph and two sons of Manasseh in vv. 20 and 27, producing a total of 75.

**Note 7 — v. 26: "sixty-six" vs. v. 27: "seventy"**
The MT distinguishes carefully: 66 persons "who came from his own body" (excluding Joseph, his two sons already in Egypt, and Jacob himself) vs. 70 as the total "persons of the house of Jacob." The arithmetic: 66 + Jacob + Joseph + Manasseh + Ephraim = 70. This is the canonical "seventy nations" number, echoing the Table of Nations (Gen 10). "Seventy" is therefore theologically loaded: a complete people.

**Note 8 — v. 28: "to show the way" (lehorot lefanav)**
The verb hora (hiphil of yrh) means "to direct, to instruct, to show the path." Judah, who offered himself as surety for Benjamin in ch. 44, is now the vanguard of Israel into Egypt. His role as pathfinder here anticipates the messianic prominence of the tribe of Judah throughout the later narrative.

**Note 9 — v. 29: "a good while" (od — עוֹד)**
The adverb 'od normally means "still, again, yet." Here it functions idiomatically: "he wept upon his neck still/yet" — indicating sustained, extended weeping. English idiom requires some elaboration; "a good while" (following a tradition from Tyndale and KJV) captures the prolonged nature of the embrace without over-dramatizing. The reunion is wordless on both sides for this extended moment.

**Note 10 — v. 30: "Now let me die" (amutah ha-pa'am)**
Jacob's statement is not suicidal despair but the language of fulfilled longing: "I can die now" — I have seen what I feared I would never see. The particle ha-pa'am ("this time," "now") emphasizes the moment of completion. It echoes Simeon's words in the NT (Luke 2:29, "Now you are releasing your servant in peace"). "Now let me die" preserves the first-person volitional force of the MT.

**Note 11 — v. 34: "abomination to Egypt" (to'evat Mitzrayim)**
The noun to'evah (taboo, abomination) was previously used for the Egyptians refusing to eat with Hebrews (43:32). Here it applies to shepherds and shepherding (ro'eh tson — "shepherd of flocks") as a profession. The reason is debated: Egyptian sources show complex attitudes toward pastoral peoples, often identifying them with the Hyksos invaders. Whatever the precise socio-historical cause, the text uses to'evah to explain the geographic separation (Goshen) that God's providence had already ordained. This separation also preserves the Israelites from cultural absorption during the Egyptian sojourn.

**Note 12 — v. 3: "Do not be afraid to go down" — the theological weight of descent**
The verb yared ("go down") runs through this chapter: Jacob goes down (vv. 3-4), God goes down with him (v. 4), Joseph "went up" (v. 29) to meet him. The geographical-theological axis of ascent and descent is central to the Exodus narrative that follows. Egypt is always "down" in the biblical worldview; Canaan and Jerusalem are always "up." God's willingness to "go down" with Jacob signals that the divine presence is not fixed to the land but accompanies the people even into exile.

---

## LXX (Vaticanus) Variants

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus / Codex B) | Significance |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------|
| v. 1 | "the God of his father Isaac" | "the God of his father Isaac" | Agreement; no variant |
| v. 10 | Sons of Simeon: Ohad listed | LXX omits Ohad; adds Jachin as "Jarib" | Minor name variant; Ohad absent in later census (Num 26) |
| v. 13 | Sons of Issachar: Jashub (yashub) | LXX: "Iasub" (Ἰασούβ) | Transliteration difference only |
| v. 16 | Sons of Gad: Ziphion (tsiphyon) | LXX: "Saphon" (Σαφών) | Metathesis or alternate reading |
| v. 20 | Joseph's sons: Manasseh and Ephraim only | LXX adds Machir son of Manasseh, and Gilead son of Machir; also adds Shuthelah and Taam and Edom, sons of Ephraim | +5 persons added to Joseph's line |
| v. 21 | Sons of Benjamin: 10 listed | LXX lists differently; includes Bela's sons Ard and Naaman as grandsons | Rearrangement shifting Ard and Naaman one generation down |
| v. 22 | Rachel's line total: 14 | LXX: 18 (with additions) | Inflated by expanded Joseph genealogy |
| v. 26 | "sixty-six persons" | LXX: "sixty-six persons" | Agreement here |
| v. 27 | "all the persons of the house of Jacob who came to Egypt: seventy" | LXX: "seventy-five" (ἑβδομήκοντα πέντε) | The famous 70 vs. 75 discrepancy |
| v. 27 | "sons of Joseph...two persons" | LXX: "nine persons" (with the additions of v. 20) | LXX adds 7 to Joseph's Egyptian descendants |
| v. 28 | "to show the way before him to Goshen" | LXX: "to meet him at the city of Heroes, in the land of Rameses" | LXX makes Goshen = Rameses territory; "Hero" = Heroopolis |
| v. 29 | "went up to meet Israel his father in Goshen" | LXX: "went up to meet Israel his father in Heroopolis" | LXX geographically specifies; same theological meaning |
| v. 34 | "every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt" | LXX: "every shepherd of sheep is an abomination to Egyptians" | LXX specifies "sheep" (probaton); slight clarification |

---

## Structural and Theological Notes

**The 70 vs. 75 Discrepancy — A Critical Note**

The MT total of 70 (shiv'im nefesh) is theologically resonant: 70 is the number of the nations listed in the Table of Nations (Gen 10), signaling that Israel-as-seventy is the covenant counterpart to the world's seventy peoples. It is also the number of elders appointed by Moses (Num 11:16) and the symbolic number of completeness in Second Temple tradition.

The LXX total of 75 is reached by adding, in vv. 20 and 27, five Egyptian-born descendants of Joseph: Machir son of Manasseh, Gilead son of Machir (grandsons), and Shuthelah, Taam, and Edom, sons of Ephraim. These names do appear in later Israelite tradition (Num 26:28-37; 1 Chr 7:14-27) but as later genealogical entries. The LXX translator — or the Vorlage he used — appears to have harmonized forward, incorporating descendants known from later tribal lists into the Egyptian migration count.

Stephen's speech in Acts 7:14 cites "seventy-five persons" (hebdomēkonta pente psychas), following the LXX. This demonstrates the LXX's authority in the early church and shows Luke's source text. Neither count is "wrong" — they represent two legitimate traditions with different counting conventions.

**The Last Theophany to a Patriarch**

Genesis 46:2-4 is the final divine speech addressed personally to one of the three patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob) in the book of Genesis. After this, God is referenced and invoked but does not speak again until Moses at the burning bush (Exod 3). This makes Jacob's "Here I am" (hinneni) the last patriarchal hinneni of Genesis — a formal close to the era of direct patriarchal revelation.

The divine speech accomplishes three things: (1) it authorizes the Egyptian descent ("do not be afraid to go down"), transforming what might be seen as flight or defeat into obedient response; (2) it renews the national promise ("I will make you into a great nation there") — the great nation promised to Abraham (Gen 12:2) will be formed not in Canaan but in Egypt; (3) it promises personal divine accompaniment and personal return — "I myself" (anokhi) is the guarantee.

**The Shepherd-Abomination and the Theology of Separation**

Joseph's instructions in vv. 31-34 are often read as pragmatic diplomacy: tell Pharaoh you are shepherds so he will settle you in Goshen away from the Egyptian heartland. But the theology runs deeper. The to'evah of shepherding for Egyptians enforces the very separation that preserves Israel's identity during four centuries in Egypt. Had Israel been absorbed into Egyptian society, there would be no Exodus, no people. The abomination of Egyptians becomes the providential instrument of Israel's preservation. This is the same pattern as Joseph's suffering: what seems like rejection and degradation is in fact divine orchestration.

**Jacob/Israel and the Completion of the Toledot**

The genealogy of vv. 8-27 is framed by the heading "these are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt" (v. 8) — a form that will echo in the opening of Exodus ("Now these are the names," Exod 1:1). The register functions as a bridge document, sealing the patriarchal era and opening the national-historical era. The seventy who go down become the "tens of thousands" (Num 10:36) who come back up — the exponential multiplication promised to Abraham (Gen 15:5) happening in the darkness of Egypt.
